categories.title: Must Do
categories.description: The essential Barcelona—Gaudí's masterpieces, the medieval maze, and the market that feeds them all.

items.description: Gaudí's still-rising basilica—light through forest columns, with the Torre de Jesucrist completing in 2026 to crown it Europe's tallest church.
items.optimal_time: First morning slot or late afternoon for the Nativity façade light; book weeks ahead

items.activity: Get lost in the Gothic Quarter
items.description: Roman walls, the cathedral cloister's geese, and lamplit medieval lanes opening onto hidden plaças.
items.optimal_time: Early morning for empty lanes; evening for atmosphere

items.description: Gaudí's mosaic dragon, the serpentine trencadís bench, and city-to-sea views from the hilltop terraces.
items.optimal_time: First entry slot of the day; timed tickets sell out—book ahead

items.description: The great iron-roofed market off La Rambla—jamón, just-squeezed juices, and counter stools at Pinotxo-style bars.
items.optimal_time: Weekday mornings before 11am; closed Sundays

categories.title: Best in Summer
categories.description: Prime months: June–August. Beach mornings, open-air screens, and street festivals that run past midnight.

items.activity: Beach day from Barceloneta to Bogatell
items.description: Swim, stroll the seafront promenade, and finish with a cold drink at a chiringuito on the sand—Bogatell is the calmer local pick.
items.optimal_time: Mornings before the crowds; June–September for warm water

items.description: Sala Montjuïc screens classics under the stars in the castle moat—bring a picnic and arrive for the live band beforehand.
items.optimal_time: Late June–early August evenings; book online

items.description: For a week around 15 August, Gràcia's residents transform their streets into elaborate handmade worlds—concerts, correfocs, and all-night celebration.
items.optimal_time: Mid-August; go early evening to see the decorated streets before the crush

items.activity: Golden hour at the Bunkers del Carmel
items.description: The old anti-aircraft battery on Turó de la Rovira has the best 360° panorama in the city—Sagrada Família to the sea.
items.optimal_time: Late afternoon into golden hour; evening access is now restricted—check closing times before planning a sunset

categories.title: Best in Winter
categories.description: Mild, blue-skied months of festive firas, fountain shows, and long Catalan feasts.

items.description: The 1929 fountain's music-and-light spectacle is back (shows resumed in late 2025)—water columns dancing beneath the MNAC's dome.
items.optimal_time: Thu–Sat evenings in winter (Wed–Sun in summer); check current schedule

items.activity: Fira de Santa Llúcia Christmas market
items.description: Barcelona's 200-plus-year-old Christmas fair beside the cathedral—hunt for a caganer and a caga tió among the greenery stalls.
items.optimal_time: Late November–23 December; weekday afternoons are calmest

items.description: The great Catalan winter ritual: charred calçots dipped in romesco, bibs on, followed by grilled meats and crema catalana.
items.optimal_time: January–March; Sundays with a reservation

items.activity: Thick hot chocolate at a granja
items.description: Duck into a century-old granja on Carrer Petritxol for a suís—spoon-thick xocolata under whipped cream, with melindros for dunking.
items.optimal_time: Chilly afternoons, November–February

categories.title: Rainy Day Favorites
categories.description: Modernisme interiors, museum treasures, and covered marvels for the rare grey day.

items.description: Five medieval palaces holding the definitive collection of Picasso's formative years, including the complete Las Meninas series.
items.optimal_time: Weekday mornings; free slots Thursday evenings and first Sundays book out fast

items.description: Domènech i Montaner's concert hall is Modernisme at full volume—an inverted stained-glass skylight dripping light onto the stage.
items.optimal_time: Guided tours through the day; better yet, book an evening concert

items.description: Gaudí's dragon-backed house on Passeig de Gràcia, now with immersive AR layers—bone balconies outside, an underwater stairwell within.
items.optimal_time: First slot of the day or evening 'Magic Nights'; book online

items.description: With Camp Nou mid-renovation, the club's trophy-stacked museum and immersive experience carry the torch for football pilgrims.
items.optimal_time: Non-match weekdays; book online and check what the renovation currently allows

categories.title: Local Experiences
categories.description: Everyday Barcelona: vermut hour, human towers, €1 pintxos, and Sunday mornings at the market.

items.activity: Vermut hour at an old bodega
items.description: Join the pre-lunch ritual: house vermouth on tap, an orange slice, olives and conservas in a barrel-lined bodega in Gràcia or Sants.
items.optimal_time: Weekends, noon–2pm

items.description: Teams in sashes stack themselves nine storeys high while a gralla pipes—spine-tingling, and utterly Catalan.
items.optimal_time: Festa majors and Sunday performances, roughly June–November; La Mercè in September is the showcase

items.activity: Pintxo crawl along Carrer de Blai
items.description: Poble-sec's pedestrian strip of bars piled with €1–€2 pintxos—grab a plate, keep the toothpicks, and settle up at the end.
items.optimal_time: Evenings from 7pm; liveliest Thursday–Saturday

items.activity: Sunday book market at Mercat de Sant Antoni
items.description: The restored iron market hall's Sunday ritual: stalls of secondhand books, comics and vinyl outside, brunch and vermut in the bars around it.
items.optimal_time: Sunday mornings, 8:30am–2pm
